title: TypeScript Interview
description: Senior-level TypeScript interview quiz focused on advanced type system and architecture decisions

questions.question: In a strict TypeScript codebase, when is unknown a better API boundary type than any?
options: When callers must explicitly narrow or validate before use
When you want all operations to type-check without guards
When you need implicit downcasts to concrete domain models
When you want the compiler to skip control-flow analysis
questions.explanation: unknown preserves type safety at boundaries by requiring narrowing before property access, calls, or arithmetic. any bypasses the checker and can silently propagate unsound assumptions.

questions.question: Which statement best describes never in advanced TypeScript design?
options: It represents impossible values and supports exhaustiveness checks
It is a top type assignable from every type
It is equivalent to undefined in strict mode
It should be used as a replacement for void in async functions
questions.explanation: never is the bottom type. It appears when no value can exist, such as a function that always throws, and it enables exhaustive switch assertions in discriminated unions.

questions.question: Why do senior teams favor discriminated unions over broad optional-property object shapes?
options: They encode valid state transitions explicitly and improve exhaustiveness
They reduce emitted JavaScript bundle size directly
They disable the need for runtime validation
They allow structural typing to become nominal automatically
questions.explanation: Discriminated unions model finite state machines clearly and force callers to handle all cases. Optional-property bags often permit invalid intermediate states that the compiler cannot prevent.

questions.question: What is the key behavior of distributive conditional types?
options: They apply the conditional to each member of a union when the checked type is a naked type parameter
They only work for tuple types and never for unions
They are evaluated at runtime for each union member
They are disabled under strictNullChecks
questions.explanation: A conditional like T extends U ? X : Y distributes across union members when T is naked. Wrapping T in a tuple, such as [T] extends [U], disables distribution.

questions.question: In conditional types, what is a practical use of infer?
options: Extracting inner types, such as function return types or tuple elements
Forcing runtime reflection metadata emission
Overriding JavaScript closure scope inference
Declaring nominal classes without constructors
questions.explanation: infer introduces a type variable within conditional type branches so you can capture and reuse parts of another type, for example ReturnType<T> style patterns.

questions.question: Why might a senior engineer choose satisfies instead of as for config objects?
options: satisfies checks shape compatibility while preserving precise inferred literal types
satisfies erases keys not present on the target type at runtime
satisfies permits incompatible values that as would reject
satisfies is only for class inheritance constraints
questions.explanation: satisfies validates that a value conforms to a target type without widening away useful literal information. as is an assertion that can hide mismatches and weaken safety.

questions.question: What problem does exactOptionalPropertyTypes solve?
options: It distinguishes omitted properties from properties explicitly set to undefined
It makes all optional properties required in interfaces
It disables strictNullChecks for optional members
It converts optional properties into readonly members
questions.explanation: Without exactOptionalPropertyTypes, optional often behaves like value | undefined. Enabling the flag models omission more precisely and catches accidental undefined assignments.

questions.question: What does noUncheckedIndexedAccess change in day-to-day coding?
options: Index access results include undefined unless proven present
It bans use of arrays and records in strict mode
It rewrites index operations to throw runtime errors
It enables numeric index signatures on all interfaces
questions.explanation: The flag forces you to acknowledge missing keys and out-of-range indices by typing indexed access as possibly undefined, reducing a common class of production bugs.

questions.question: How should assertion functions (asserts value is T) be used in robust systems?
options: To centralize runtime checks that also narrow types for callers
To replace unit tests by relying on compile-time checks only
To convert unknown into any without validation
To force emitted JavaScript to inline branch prediction hints
questions.explanation: Assertion functions bridge runtime validation and static narrowing. They are especially valuable at I/O boundaries, deserialization, and unsafe external integrations.

questions.question: What is a major risk of overusing function overload signatures in public APIs?
options: Call-site complexity and brittle maintenance when behavior could be modeled by unions or generics
Overloads always generate extra runtime wrappers
Overloads cannot be used with exported functions
Overloads disable IntelliSense for all parameters
questions.explanation: Overloads are powerful but can become difficult to evolve and reason about. Well-designed unions, parameter objects, or generics often provide clearer and more maintainable APIs.

questions.question: When modeling finite domain values, why are string literal unions often preferred over enums?
options: They avoid runtime emit and integrate naturally with JSON payloads
They support inheritance while enums do not
They are nominal and prevent structural assignment
They are required for switch statements
questions.explanation: Literal unions are type-only and map directly to serialized strings. Enums emit runtime objects, which can be useful but may add bundle and interop complexity.

questions.question: What is a practical downside of const enum in shared libraries?
options: Inlining can break consumers across compilation boundaries and toolchains
const enum values are allocated dynamically at runtime
const enum cannot represent numeric values
const enum disables dead-code elimination
questions.explanation: const enum is erased and inlined, which can create versioning and transpiler compatibility issues for distributed packages unless all consumers compile with compatible settings.

questions.question: How does noImplicitOverride help in large OO TypeScript codebases?
options: It ensures subclass methods intentionally override base members
It allows private methods to be overridden without errors
It forces all methods to be abstract
It changes runtime prototype lookup behavior
questions.explanation: The override keyword plus noImplicitOverride catches accidental shadowing when base class members are renamed or signatures change, improving refactor safety.

questions.question: What is the senior-level rationale for useUnknownInCatchVariables?
options: Thrown values are not guaranteed to be Error objects, so narrowing is required
It guarantees stack traces for all caught values
It converts all thrown strings into Error automatically
It removes need for custom error classes
questions.explanation: JavaScript permits throwing any value. Typing catch bindings as unknown forces explicit normalization and avoids unsafe assumptions like direct err.message access.

questions.question: Why are branded types used in critical domain modeling?
options: To simulate nominal distinctions between structurally identical primitives
To create runtime validation without code
To speed up object allocation in V8
To enable automatic JSON schema generation
questions.explanation: Branding allows differentiation of types like UserId and OrderId even if both are strings, preventing accidental mixing in APIs that rely on structural typing.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes variance pressure in callback-heavy APIs?
options: Parameter positions are contravariant in sound systems, so narrower callbacks are unsafe substitutes
Return positions are always contravariant
Variance does not matter in TypeScript due to structural typing
All generic types are invariant by default
questions.explanation: A callback expecting a more specific parameter cannot safely replace one expecting a broader parameter. Understanding variance avoids subtle assignment bugs in framework and library APIs.

questions.question: What does the built-in Awaited<T> utility model accurately?
options: The recursively unwrapped resolved type of Promise-like values
Only the first level of Promise<T> without nested unwrapping
The runtime value after await executes
A union of pending and fulfilled states
questions.explanation: Awaited<T> mirrors await behavior by recursively unwrapping Promise-like types, which helps model async API composition more precisely.

questions.question: When should a senior developer reach for template literal types?
options: When encoding structured string contracts such as event names, route keys, or cache IDs
When needing faster runtime string concatenation
When replacing all union types in a codebase
Only when generating declaration files
questions.explanation: Template literal types can encode naming patterns and catch invalid string construction at compile time, especially in event-driven and API-heavy systems.

questions.question: What is a common anti-pattern with as const in application code?
options: Using it to silence type errors instead of modeling intent with proper types
Using it on readonly tuple literals
Using it for discriminant fields
Using it in lookup tables for narrow key inference
questions.explanation: as const is useful for literal inference and immutability intent. Misusing it as a blanket fix can mask design issues and create inflexible types where widening is appropriate.

questions.question: Why might a library expose unknown instead of generic defaults like any in parse functions?
options: To force consumers to provide validators or explicit narrowing before use
To improve runtime parse throughput
To avoid emitting declaration files
To make JSON parsing nominally typed
questions.explanation: Returning unknown communicates that data shape is untrusted until validated. This promotes safer integration patterns, especially with external or versioned payloads.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of project references in monorepos?
options: Incremental, dependency-aware builds with clear package boundaries
Automatic runtime isolation between packages
Elimination of all path alias configuration
Guaranteed zero-cost cross-package imports
questions.explanation: Project references enable tsc to understand package dependency graphs and reuse build artifacts, improving large-repo build performance and architecture discipline.

questions.question: Why is isolatedModules important when using transpilers like SWC or Babel?
options: It enforces patterns that can be compiled safely one file at a time
It enables runtime module federation
It disables declaration file generation
It guarantees tree-shaking of unused exports
questions.explanation: Single-file transpilers cannot use whole-program type information. isolatedModules catches constructs that require cross-file analysis and could miscompile otherwise.

questions.question: What does verbatimModuleSyntax primarily protect against?
options: Unexpected import/export rewriting that can blur type-only versus value semantics
All circular dependencies at runtime
Duplicate symbol declarations across packages
Invalid JSX pragma configuration
questions.explanation: Keeping module syntax verbatim helps align TypeScript output with modern bundlers and ESM expectations, reducing surprises around elided or transformed imports.

questions.question: Which choice best captures declaration merging tradeoffs?
options: Useful for ecosystem augmentation but dangerous if overused due to hidden global coupling
It is runtime inheritance for classes across packages
It can only merge interfaces declared in one file
It replaces module augmentation completely
questions.explanation: Declaration merging powers patterns like extending framework types, but it can obscure ownership boundaries and cause surprising global effects if not tightly controlled.

questions.question: When augmenting third-party module types, what practice is most robust?
options: Keep augmentations minimal, version-aware, and colocated with integration tests
Overwrite all exported types to match local preferences
Place augmentations in random feature files for convenience
Rely on skipLibCheck to avoid maintenance
questions.explanation: Module augmentation should be explicit and test-backed because upstream type changes can break assumptions. Minimal surface area reduces upgrade risk.

questions.question: Why are user-defined type guards preferable to repeated inline casts?
options: They centralize validation logic and provide reusable, trustworthy narrowing
They execute faster than plain JavaScript conditionals
They remove the need for runtime checks
They are required for generic functions
questions.explanation: Type guards encode domain checks once and allow safe narrowing everywhere they are used, improving consistency and reducing assertion-driven unsoundness.

questions.question: What does keyof on a union type represent?
options: The union of property keys from each member
Only keys common to all members
The intersection of value types across members
A runtime array of enumerable keys
questions.explanation: keyof (A | B) yields keys from both members. Property access still requires narrowing because not every key is safe on every member at a given control-flow point.

questions.question: What is the safest interpretation of Record<string, T> in API contracts?
options: A potentially sparse dictionary where any key lookup may be absent
A map guaranteed to contain all possible string keys
An object with numeric-only keys
A sealed object with exact key set known at compile time
questions.explanation: Record<string, T> describes key and value domains, not key presence guarantees. In strict code, missing entries should be considered and handled explicitly.

questions.question: Why can DeepPartial-style utilities be risky in write APIs?
options: They can permit semantically invalid partial states that violate invariants
They always increase runtime memory usage
They prevent usage of optional chaining
They are incompatible with mapped types
questions.explanation: Deep partial updates are convenient but can erode domain guarantees. Mature systems pair them with explicit patch schemas and validation rules to preserve invariants.

questions.question: In advanced generics, why is constraining T extends object sometimes insufficient?
options: Because object includes arrays/functions and may be too broad for intended key/value operations
Because object excludes interfaces
Because object guarantees index signatures
Because object forces nominal typing
questions.explanation: object is often broader than desired. Senior APIs usually constrain using Record constraints, keyof relations, or callable signatures that match actual requirements.

questions.question: What is a robust pattern for exhaustive handling in reducers or state machines?
options: Switch on discriminant and route default to a never-typed assertion
Use default branch returning null for unknown cases
Cast action to any in each case branch
Rely on runtime logs to detect missing branches
questions.explanation: A never assertion in the default branch forces compile-time errors when new union members are added but not handled, making refactors safer.

questions.question: How should Promise.all be typed mentally in production code reviews?
options: As preserving tuple positions and resolved element types for const tuples
As always returning Promise<any[]>
As flattening nested arrays recursively at runtime and type level
As rejecting only after all promises settle
questions.explanation: With tuple inputs, Promise.all preserves positional typing, enabling strongly typed destructuring of heterogeneous async dependencies.

questions.question: What is a maintainable way to expose polymorphic APIs without overload explosion?
options: Use discriminated parameter objects plus generics for result coupling
Create one overload per possible runtime value
Return any and document behavior in comments only
Use conditional runtime eval to infer static types
questions.explanation: Parameter objects with discriminants scale better than many overloads and make backward-compatible evolution easier while preserving strong inference.

questions.question: Why is preserving literal inference in domain constants important?
options: It enables tighter unions and prevents invalid values across boundaries
It reduces runtime memory by deduplicating strings
It forces JavaScript engines to intern objects
It disables module side effects
questions.explanation: Literal inference turns constants into usable type sources for contracts, routing, and event names, reducing drift between values and type definitions.

questions.question: What is a correct senior perspective on skipLibCheck?
options: It can speed builds but may hide type conflicts in dependencies
It guarantees third-party type correctness
It should always be enabled in libraries
It removes the need for lockfile management
questions.explanation: skipLibCheck is a performance tradeoff. It can reduce friction, but unresolved declaration mismatches may surface later as runtime or integration issues.

questions.question: What does declaration: true primarily provide for package maintainers?
options: Generated .d.ts files that define the public type contract for consumers
Runtime schema validation in production
Automatic semantic versioning enforcement
Inlining of generic types into emitted JavaScript
questions.explanation: Declaration emit is essential for typed package consumption. It formalizes the API surface and helps consumers get accurate IntelliSense and compile-time checks.

questions.question: In library design, what is a danger of exporting overly complex conditional types directly?
options: Consumer compile performance and error readability can degrade significantly
They are ignored by the TypeScript compiler in declaration files
They cannot interoperate with unions
They force transpilation to CommonJS
questions.explanation: Type-level complexity has a real cost. Deeply nested conditionals can slow editor tooling and produce opaque errors, harming developer experience at scale.

questions.question: Why should runtime validation still exist in strongly typed TypeScript systems?
options: TypeScript types are erased at runtime and cannot verify external inputs
TypeScript automatically validates only primitive values
Runtime validation is needed only in development builds
Compilation emits guards for every interface by default
questions.explanation: TypeScript improves static correctness but does not enforce data shape at runtime. External data sources must still be validated to ensure system safety.

questions.question: What is a practical reason to prefer unknown over generic T in untrusted parse helpers?
options: T can imply false certainty when no runtime evidence exists for that shape
unknown prevents all future type narrowing
Generic T runs faster than unknown at runtime
T forces declaration merging in consumers
questions.explanation: Returning arbitrary T from unvalidated parsing is unsound because callers can choose any type argument. unknown correctly communicates uncertainty until validated.

questions.question: How should optional chaining be viewed in code review for nullability-heavy logic?
options: As a readability aid, not a substitute for explicit domain-level null handling
As proof that values are always non-null
As equivalent to runtime schema validation
As a way to silence strictNullChecks errors safely
questions.explanation: Optional chaining avoids crashes on access but can also hide missing-state bugs. Senior code distinguishes benign absence from invariant violations explicitly.

questions.question: What does this parameter typing in functions primarily enable?
options: Explicit control of call-site this context without affecting runtime parameters
Automatic binding of methods to instances
Runtime validation of this shape by emitted code
Inheritance of private fields across modules
questions.explanation: A fake first parameter named this is type-only. It lets APIs model expected invocation context, especially in callback and utility function design.

questions.question: What is a safe way to migrate legacy any-heavy modules incrementally?
options: Replace broad any with unknown at boundaries, then add focused guards and assertions
Enable all strict flags and rewrite everything in one commit
Cast all exports to never to force compiler success
Disable noImplicitAny permanently
questions.explanation: Incremental migration works best by shrinking unsafe surfaces gradually. Boundary hardening plus targeted narrowing yields steady risk reduction without stopping delivery.

questions.question: Why are path aliases alone not a substitute for package boundaries in monorepos?
options: They improve import ergonomics but do not enforce architectural ownership or API contracts
They force circular dependencies to fail at compile time
They guarantee independent package versioning
They generate declaration maps automatically
questions.explanation: Aliases are syntactic convenience. Strong boundaries come from package structure, references, lint rules, and explicit public entry points.

questions.question: What is the primary value of declarationMap in published libraries?
options: It improves editor navigation from .d.ts back to source definitions
It compiles source maps for production runtime errors only
It removes need for source maps in JavaScript output
It enforces API compatibility across versions
questions.explanation: Declaration maps enhance developer experience by allowing go-to-definition and debugging workflows to trace type declarations back to original source.

questions.question: In typed event bus design, what is a robust approach?
options: Map event names to payload types via indexed access and generic constraints
Use string keys with any payload for flexibility
Represent all payloads as unknown[] tuples
Generate event types from runtime logs
questions.explanation: An EventMap pattern ties each event key to a payload contract, enabling compile-time safety for emit and subscribe operations with minimal runtime overhead.

questions.question: Why can widening literal types too early hurt API ergonomics?
options: It loses precise information needed for inference and discriminated unions
It increases runtime object allocation
It disables control-flow analysis entirely
It prevents tree-shaking of unused code
questions.explanation: Premature widening turns specific contracts into generic strings or numbers, reducing compiler assistance and making invalid states harder to catch.

questions.question: What is a strong reason to avoid pervasive non-null assertions (!) in mature codebases?
options: They bypass null safety and can hide real invariant violations
They slow TypeScript compilation significantly
They always throw at runtime when value is null
They prevent strict mode from being enabled
questions.explanation: Non-null assertions are escape hatches. Overuse erodes strictness guarantees and often signals missing control-flow modeling or boundary validation.

questions.question: When designing generic repositories or services, what is a key constraint strategy?
options: Constrain type parameters to required capabilities rather than broad placeholder types
Leave all type parameters unconstrained for maximum flexibility
Replace generics with any in implementation files
Use extends never to defer errors to runtime
questions.explanation: Meaningful constraints encode assumptions explicitly and improve error messages. Overly loose generics reduce safety and make misuse easier.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between interface extension and intersection in conflict scenarios?
options: Intersections can produce impossible property types, while interface extension surfaces conflicts earlier
Interfaces always create runtime wrappers
Intersections cannot include function members
Interface extension is limited to classes
questions.explanation: Intersecting incompatible property types can yield never-like impossible fields. Interface extension generally reports incompatible redeclarations more directly.

questions.question: What does noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature encourage?
options: Explicit indexed access for dictionary-like shapes to reflect uncertainty
Automatic key existence checks for dot access
Replacement of index signatures with enums
Runtime proxy validation for all object reads
questions.explanation: The flag makes dictionary access intent explicit and reduces accidental dot-access assumptions on loosely keyed objects.

questions.question: Why should tslib and emit strategy be considered in large TypeScript apps?
options: Helper emit choices affect bundle size, duplication, and compatibility
They only impact type-check speed and nothing else
tslib is required only for Node.js and not browsers
Helper emit has no effect when using modern bundlers
questions.explanation: Downlevel helper strategy influences generated output characteristics. Centralizing helpers can reduce duplication and improve consistency across packages.

questions.question: What is a senior-level benefit of separating domain types from transport DTO types?
options: It prevents external schema drift from leaking directly into core business invariants
It eliminates need for data mappers
It guarantees zero runtime transformation cost
It allows TypeScript to validate database constraints
questions.explanation: Transport and domain have different stability and semantics. Explicit mapping preserves domain integrity and localizes changes from external contracts.

questions.question: Why are recursive conditional types sometimes constrained with depth parameters?
options: To avoid excessive instantiation complexity and compiler performance issues
To enable runtime tail-call optimization
To preserve nominal typing under unions
To prevent declaration file generation
questions.explanation: Deep recursive types can hit instantiation limits and slow tooling. Depth-limited patterns provide practical safety while preserving useful expressiveness.

questions.question: What is an appropriate use of Extract<T, U>?
options: Selecting union members assignable to U for precise subtyping
Removing keys from object types by name
Converting tuple types into arrays
Inferring runtime values from interfaces
questions.explanation: Extract filters union constituents by assignability, making it useful for deriving sub-unions by shape or discriminant.

questions.question: What is an appropriate use of Exclude<T, U>?
options: Removing union members assignable to U to model remaining possibilities
Removing object properties with optional modifiers
Deleting runtime fields before serialization
Converting unions into intersections
questions.explanation: Exclude is the counterpart to Extract and is commonly used to model unhandled cases or prune broad unions into allowed subsets.

questions.question: In strongly typed API clients, why couple endpoint keys to response types via mapped types?
options: It enables compile-time correctness across request construction and response handling
It removes the need for HTTP error handling
It guarantees backend schema compatibility forever
It compiles endpoints into runtime routers automatically
questions.explanation: Typed endpoint maps reduce mismatch errors and improve refactorability by connecting each operation to its precise request and response contracts.

questions.question: What is a robust strategy for modeling versioned message contracts in TypeScript?
options: Use discriminated unions with explicit version tags and migration boundaries
Keep one interface and append optional fields indefinitely
Represent every version as any for backward compatibility
Depend on JSON.parse to infer the newest schema
questions.explanation: Version tags make evolution explicit and allow safe branching, validation, and migration logic while preserving backward compatibility guarantees.

questions.question: Why should senior developers review type-level API error messages as a design signal?
options: Because unreadable errors often indicate overcomplex abstractions that hurt adoption
Because compiler errors do not affect maintainability
Because complex errors always mean stronger type safety
Because error clarity is only an IDE concern
questions.explanation: Type safety and developer experience are both product concerns. If consumers cannot interpret errors quickly, APIs become costly to use and maintain.

questions.question: What is a key governance practice for shared TypeScript utility types in large organizations?
options: Curate a small, documented utility surface with ownership and deprecation policy
Allow each team to publish overlapping utilities without review
Use internal any-based helpers to avoid breaking changes
Freeze all utility types to prevent evolution
questions.explanation: A governed utility layer prevents duplication and inconsistency. Ownership, docs, and lifecycle policy keep abstractions useful rather than accidental complexity.

questions.question: When evaluating a senior TypeScript candidate, which signal is strongest?
options: They balance type-system power with runtime realities, maintainability, and team ergonomics
They can write the most deeply nested conditional types possible
They avoid runtime validation because TypeScript is enough
They prefer type assertions over modeling constraints
questions.explanation: Senior TypeScript skill is not just advanced syntax. It is making architecture decisions that improve correctness, delivery speed, and long-term system maintainability.
